title: Bad branding doesn't kill a business. It taxes it.
fbPost:
Walked down my local street today and actually looked at the shopfronts.

Some of the branding was rough. Not charming rough. More 'someone's sister found clip art in 1998' rough.

And some of these places have been trading for years.

So here's the honest bit: a good logo won't save a bad business. But bad branding still costs you. It just sends the bill somewhere you can't see it.

The customer who felt something was off and walked past doesn't send a rejection email. You never meet them.

The fish and chip shop survives a bad logo because everyone knows what they're buying. A cosmetic clinic or a builder asking for a deposit? Different game entirely.
linkedinPost:
A bad logo won't kill a business. It taxes it, and the bill goes somewhere the owner never sees.

I walked my local street today and half the shopfronts had branding that looked like clip art with rent. Some have traded for years. So clearly it isn't fatal.

But look closer at who survives it. The fish and chip shop, the corner store, the known local with a low-risk, obvious purchase. Everything else is strong enough to carry a weak first impression.

Now change the ask. A cosmetic clinic wants a stranger to trust them near their face. A builder wants a renovation deposit before a nail goes in. A consultant wants premium rates. Those customers are already nervous, and a cheap-looking front door tells them to stay nervous.

The test was never whether the logo is pretty. It's whether your first impression matches the level of trust you're asking for. Good enough branding stays good enough right up until you go premium, go new, or chase a different customer. Then it quietly argues against everything else you've improved, and nobody tells you why.
